data.title: Hovnanian: Benefits From Improved Sentiment Towards Homebuilders, But Risks Remain
data.description:
Underperforming In 2025 As A Whole, But Improvements Seen Of Late

Hovnanian Enterprises, Inc. (NYSE:HOV), which has been selling homes across the U.S. (currently present in 13 states) for six-and-a-half decades, hasn’t been experiencing the most rewarding of years so far. On a YTD basis, the stock is up by a late-single-digit threshold, and is underperforming other homebuilders and stocks from the broader markets by around 120-150bps.

Admittedly HOV’s performance since the final week of July has been a lot more heartening, and it is certainly performing a lot better than when we last reviewed it at the start of July (when it was underperforming other homebuilders by 4x on a YTD basis).

Now, last week, HOV released its Q3 earnings (the company follows an October year ending fiscal), and the reaction since has been very mixed. On the 21 st of August, when results came out, HOV ended up losing ground by -16%, before closing the day -12% lower. However, the 22nd of August witnessed a very different outcome, with the HOV stock bouncing back very strongly by +11% (positive broader market sentiment following the news of a likelihood of a September rate cut, certainly played a part). All in all, HOV is effectively down by only -2% post results.

Given a rather volatile post-earnings reaction, how should investors view the Q3 earnings, and what next for the HOV stock?

Q3 Earnings- A Damaging Bottomline Performance, Relative To Market Expectations

On the earnings headline front, HOV disappointed in a big way, particularly at the GAAP-EPS line. As against consensus estimates of $3.51, HOV ended up delivering a figure ($1.99) which translated to a massive short-fall of 57% (On a YoY basis, the Q3 reported EPS also represented a contraction of 80%)!

One of the main contributors for the weak bottom line performance is the degree of inventory impairments and land option write-offs that Hovnanian is currently incurring (it was up by 5.2x YoY in Q3, and up by 5.6x YoY for the first 9 months of the current fiscal). For the uninitiated, HOV reports under three segments- the Northeast segment, the East Coast segment, and the West Coast segment. As things stand, most of the pressure and the associated impairments and land write-offs are coming from the Westcoast segment, which accounts for their second largest land position.

On the topline too, HOV fell short of Street estimates again (for the third successive quarter), but at least the cadence of the short-fall was not as bad as what was seen previously this year. As opposed to mid-single-digit percentage shortfalls seen in Q1, and Q2 of 2025, the Q3 miss was less than 1%. Also, the reported figure of $801M, was roughly in line with the mid-point of management’s guidance of $750M-$850M.

The Usual Q4 Uplift In Revenue Will Fail To Take Place This Year

A few investors may be happy to note that even though HOV’s revenue was in line with the mid-point of the guidance, it still represented a pick up in the YoY growth rate to a double-digit threshold of nearly 11% after a decline seen in Q2.

While the sub-11% growth rate in Q3 is encouraging to see (more so as it is almost double the average sales growth rate per quarter seen over the last 5 years), investors should be aware that management is essentially still guiding to the same $750-$850M worth of sales in Q4-25 as well Another $800M worth of quarterly sales (essentially flat sales vs. Q3) is nothing to write home about, when you consider that Q4 has traditionally been their seasonally strongest quarter (in the previous two fiscals, the improvement seen from Q3 to Q4 has averaged 35-36% QoQ).

Even on a YoY basis, sales are poised to witness a massive contraction of -16% YoY (from the levels of $957M seen last year). Also expect consensus to make further downward adjustments by around 1% as the Q4 figure is currently at$808M. Looking ahead, it is expected that HOV will likely only deliver YoY sales growth improvement from Q2-26 onwards.

Management also didn’t sound the most optimistic in their commentary, noting that the “market environment remains challenging” and “affordability challenges are weighing on buyer activity,” something we had touched upon in our previous article.

Gross Margin Pressures May Bottom Out By Q4-25

In addition to red flags linked to the homebuilding market and the affordability quotient, management also flagged challenges linked to mortgage rates, but we suspect at least on this regard, they may get a breather soon enough. In fact we believe this was one of the primary reasons why the HOV stock and other homebuilders (both the prime homebuilding ETFs- ITB and XHB were up by over 5% each on Friday) rose quite strongly.

Over the last few quarters, a lot of the homebuilders have been trying to galvanize sales volumes, and clear the order backlog, by engaging in a higher cadence of mortgage rate buydowns (in HOV’s words they are focusing on “pace versus price”). This is essentially a form of incentive whereby homebuilders like HOV pay fees to lenders (in this case-K Hovnanian Mortgage, their banking subsidiary) to help subsidize the buyer’s monthly mortgage payments. 75% of HOV’s homebuyers (both in Q2 and Q3) have taken advantage of this option, and while it abets them, this concession is a hit for HOV, and ends up weakening their gross margin profile. Besides mortgage rate buydowns (which are the largest), HOV is also engaging in other incentives, and overall they are currently accounting of 11.6% of the average sales price (if you want to get a sense of how rapidly this is growing, note that just a quarter ago, it stood at 10.5%, and a year ago it stood at just 7.7%). Because of that gross margins that were around the 22% mark have been hovering at the 17% mark, and are poised to drop to 15.8% (the mid-point of management’s gross margin guidance) by Q4.

Following Friday’s annual Fed conference, expectations are now on for 50bps worth of policy rate cuts before the year end. While this may or boost prospective home owners’ desire to own a home (after all there are other affordability challenges beyond just lower interest rates), it should almost certainly put less pressure on HOV’s gross margins, as there will be a lesser degree of subsidization.

SG&A Expense Margin To Rise In Q4 After A Decline In Q3

Despite the sequential improvement in sales, we were happy to note that the SG&A did not rise, which shows a good level of operating leverage; in fact, from levels of 11.7% of sales in Q2 25, the SG&A declined to 11.3%. However, the expectation for Q4 is a spike again to the 11.5%. Given that we are expecting flattish sales of $800M in Q4, and weaker gross profits, further bottom line pressure can be expected in Q4. One of the prime reasons why the SG&A margin looks set to increase is because HOV is expanding hiring ahead of a ramp up in community count growth.

What Do The Valuations Look Like?

The last time we covered HOV, it was priced at only a single digit P/E multiple and a 25% discount to other homebuilders. Now following the HOV’s appreciation, without any upward revision in the forward EPS, valuations now work out to a multiple of over 12.1x. While this still represents a discount to other homebuilders, it is a lot narrower at only 18%. Crucially, HOV is no longer priced at a discount to its own rolling 5-year average of 10.34x.

Do also consider that at a multiple of over 12.1x, you would be getting into a business that is poised to witness a contraction in its earnings base, not just in the current year, but next year as well (it is only in FY27 that YoY EPS growth will be seen again).

Closing Thoughts - What Do The Charts Suggest?

The relative strength [RS] charts of HOV vs. other homebuilders would suggest that investors who are interested in this space may not find any great incentive to either rotate into or out of HOV now. Opportunities to go long or short are typically found when the current RS is trading at a very premium or discount to its 5-year average (which is not quite the case now).

However, momentum players will be interested to note that quite unlike the last time, the stock no longer trades below its 50, 100 and 200DMA, and has recouped the latter (which is arguably the most important) after 7 months.

Investors may also take note of the developments on HOV’s larger time frame chart, which provides a bird’s eye view of things.

The last time we reviewed HOV, we saw that it was no longer forming a series of lower-lows (LLs), but also not forming a series of higher-highs (HHs). Now we can see that after the stock built a base, it has commenced an uptrend, taking support against an ascending trendline, and witnessing a series of higher-highs and lower-lows. Encouragingly, the uptrend has also been supported by a lift in weekly volumes, with the average going up from 300-400K to 650K a week. This suggests that larger participants are now getting involved in the story.

Separately, investors may also be interested to note that the cadence of insider selling has dipped in July (relative to June where there were close to$590K worth of sales) with no sales taking place this month.

All things considered, we believe investors with high-risk appetite can afford to be a bit more optimistic about HOV’s prospects, particularly with the onset of rate cuts which will boost sentiment towards homebuilders as a while. Having said that, please also be aware that Q3 results were hardly encouraging, and crucially, no major improvements are likely to be seen when the company next reports earnings in November.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Fed Seems More Bullish, But I'm Not
data.description:
Stocks experienced one heck of a rally on Friday. Things were pretty calm for the better part of last week, and we even witnessed some mild volatility increases while the Jackson Hole Symposium and other key events took shape.

S&P 500 1-Year Chart

The S&P 500 "SPX" (SP500) even experienced five down days in a row before catching a considerable bid on Friday. Of course, it wasn't just the SPX, as the "Nasdaq 100 ETF" (NASDAQ:QQQ) bounced by more than 1.5% on Friday, snapping a six day losing streak on Wall Street.

QQQ 1-Year Chart

Still, while we've seen some minor volatility increases, the pullbacks in major market averages have been quite insignificant as of late, to be honest. In fact, the SPX fell by only 2.3% and the Nasdaq 100 by roughly 4.6% during the recent shallow pullback process. These are hardly corrections that could reset frothy valuations and offset overheated technical conditions. Therefore, the market may require a more considerable pullback/consolidation process before stocks take off to new ATHs again.

Generally, in a base case scenario, I believe a retracement to about 22K in the Nasdaq 100 futures may occur, which could translate to a pullback to roughly the $540-520 range in the QQQ. I still think that a base case pullback could take the SPX to about the 6,100-6,000 support level in future weeks. "What pullback?" Some of you may be saying. Didn't the Fed just give a green light to equities? Well, not so quick. While Fed Chair Powell did signal that he was willing to look past some potential inflation and focus more on the labor market, potentially opening the door more to a rate cut in September, a September rate cut may still not be a done deal.

September Rate Cut - Still Not Guaranteed

The market liked what it heard on Friday, but I must stress that a rate cut in September is not guaranteed. There are still crucial data elements to assess, and there is still considerable uncertainty in the near term.

The probability for a rate cut increased mildly from a week ago, but the probabilities don't signal a home run here. Also, there is still potential for substantial fluctuations in the probabilities, as the FOMC meeting remains more than three weeks away. I am not confident that the Fed itself is sure whether it will cut in September or hold off for another month instead. Really, a lot will depend on the upcoming data.

This Is A Crucial Data Week

This week will be crucial for markets, as there are many critical data points to assess that may increase or decrease the probabilities for a rate cut in September.

Key Data Points This Week

Essential data points coming up this week include new homes data, durable goods orders, consumer confidence, Atlanta Fed GDPNow, oil inventories, GDP, jobs data, consumer sentiment, and of course, most importantly, the core-PCE reading on Friday. In addition, we have Fed officials speaking throughout the week. The PCE will be monitored especially closely, and it may be more challenging for the Fed to justify a rate cut in September if the PCE comes in hotter than expected this week. Also, next week, we will see the crucial nonfarm payrolls report, which may be the most significant market mover relative to rates. Also, I have to emphasize that the recent (last month's) nonfarm payrolls report was so poor that there is now growing concern that the slowdown may be worse than expected in the U.S. The past two months' downward revisions were very substantial, signaling that the economy barely created any jobs in the last three months.

Some key questions relative to the Fed also exist. What if this upcoming job number is better than expected? Will that now reduce the probabilities for a rate cut? What if we get higher PCE, CPI, and PPI inflation while seeing job numbers better than expected? Would this dynamic reduce the rate cut probability to below 50%?

On the other hand, what if the job number comes in worse than anticipated again? Yes, that could lock in a rate cut for September. However, at what point does the market say, the heck with a 25 bps rate cut? I am more worried about the labor market and the potential slowdown, or even a possible recession which may be ahead.

So, there is a very delicate balance here. The Fed must walk a fine line between inflation, the labor market, growth expectations, and other potential risks to the economy and markets in the near, intermediate, and long term.

Earnings: Still Very Important

How about this PDD earnings? About a 50% beat on earnings. That's pretty impressive, and China may be back. The China slowdown likely will not last forever. Also, the thawing trade relations with the U.S. could be a significant net positive for the Chinese economy, as well as a catalyst for improving demand for Chinese companies' shares.

However, let's focus on Nvidia (NVDA), as it will report on Wednesday, and this may be the single most important earnings report of this earnings season. Nvidia remains the undisputed AI hardware leader, and all eyes will be focused on Nvidia's results and guidance to gauge where the AI market goes next.

There's been increasing talk of bubbles and of even "AI fatigue." And I understand this argument, as AI has been largely responsible for pushing many stocks higher in recent years. In any case, all eyes will be on Nvidia, toshow that the AI rally still has steam. However, what if Nvidia guides slightly worse than expected? Or what if it guides are just not as great as the market has become accustomed to? What if Nvidia's margins dip more than anticipated? Essentially, Nvidia's stock is priced for perfection, and expectations for Nvidia and the AI market in general remain sky high. In other words, the market may be looking for excuses to take profits here, and if there is even a hiccup in Nvidia's earnings, margins, or guidance, and I believe the broader AI market could fall. On the flip side of that, Nvidia could report better than expected on all or most metrics, in which case the push higher could advance.

Nonetheless, I am becoming a bit more cautious on the AI segment in the near term here, as expectations have risen incredibly, and there is little room to disappoint. In addition to Nvidia, we will receive earnings from Dell (DELL), Marvell (MRVL), CrowdStrike (CRWD), and other crucial companies this week.

The Bottom Line

While it's constructive that the Fed appears to have adopted a more dovish stance toward future rate decreases, a September rate cut while likely, is not guaranteed. I am not convinced the FOMC has come to a consensus on lowering rates at the September meeting yet. As the Fed claims, it likely remains data-dependent, and there are still several very key data points left between now and the upcoming FOMC meeting in several weeks.

Meanwhile, many valuations remain stretched, technical conditions are quite extended, and the sentiment may still turn more negative, triggering an uptick in volatility in future weeks. We are also around a seasonally weak time for equities, as the August to November timeframe typically leads to healthy corrections to take some froth out of valuations, normalize technical conditions, and set the market up for a year-end advance.

Therefore, I still believe that a base case pullback to about 6,200–6,000 SPX could occur and would be constructive for markets, in my view. Nonetheless, despite the potential for some transitory near term turbulence, I remain bullish on markets in the intermediate and longer term, and I am keeping my year-end SPX target at 7,000.

data.title: Ameresco: Contrarian Buy With Rerating Potential
data.description:
Undervalued renewable operator trading at EPC multiples

So far in 2025, the industrials sector has been delivering very attractive returns, but Ameresco (NYSE:AMRC) is down -3% YTD as this article is being written, and it continues its slide since the 2021 highs.

The sector ETF XLI is up +14% YTD, and after analyzing the causes, I have confirmed that the market treats AMRC as a low-multiple Engineering, Procurement and Construction (EPC) contractor, surrounded by uncertainty about its ability to consistently convert backlog into cash.

My view after looking at different angles is different. In Q2, it reported revenue growing at a rate of +8%, adjusted EBITDA of +24%, and a record backlog of $5.1B. It is important to add that more than 65% of operating profit already comes from more stable projects in renewable energy and storage, instead of public EPCs that are more dependent on government contracts and lower margin.

At the current multiples of 15x EV/EBITDA, AMRC trades like a mid-cap construction and engineering company, when its revenue mix makes it closer to a renewable utility model. And currently, the market values these companies at multiples of 18x–19x, which I see as a gap given its fundamentals.

On the risk side, there are lawsuits, supply chain tensions, and difficulties in free cash flow conversion that keep the market cautious. Also worth adding is a short interest currently close to 8%.

In my opinion, if AMRC keeps margins above 15% and proves it can consistently turn backlog into cash, the first stop is to return to the $27 highs from early 2025, something feasible to achieve in the coming months.

At current prices of $24 at the time of writing, I maintain a Buy recommendation. I rely on fundamentals and also on the key technical reference of $27, which would be the first move confirming the hypothesis of a trend reversal in the stock.

Overview

Founded by civil engineer George Sakellaris, who still leads the company today as CEO, Ameresco remains a family-influenced business thanks to the more than 70% voting power its founder still holds.

The company operates across several business verticals, including energy efficiency projects for municipalities and public administrations known as Energy Savings Performance Contracts (ESPC), as well as company-owned assets in renewable natural gas (RNG), distributed solar, and battery storage (OTCQB:BESS).

While Ameresco’s core market is in the United States, in recent years it has been expanding into Canada and Europe, particularly through its company-owned biogas and energy efficiency projects.

The market often views Ameresco as an Engineering, Procurement, and Construction (EPC) contractor. However, after digging into its filings and revenue mix, I believe this is where the valuation gap lies, and where the potential for rerating could emerge.

Revenue growth, margin expansion and guidance reaffirmed

This August 2025, Ameresco presented a Q2 that for me confirms this change in trend. The reported GAAP EPS was $0.24, while normalized EPS was $0.27. Above consensus.

The 2025 guidance is reaffirmed, and growth is expected both in EPS and in revenue, which in this case reached about $472.3M, representing +7.8% YoY. Again beating consensus expectations.

Margins are where we can extract more from this Q2. The revenue mix the company is pivoting to has supported reporting adjusted EBITDA of +24% compared to the previous year, driven by a greater contribution from BESS storage and solar projects. As expected, after these results the stock reacted strongly upwards from the $16 area to the $22 area, which it has consolidated since then.

Regarding the reading of the options chain, I see some tension. In September, interest is dispersed, but in October when Q3 results will probably be published, positions are concentrated at $20, which indicates that the market expects a sharp move around those prices.

Looking to December, open contracts shift to lower strikes, with puts of $7.5–10 still alive. This may be a sign that institutions maintain the bearish hedges we have mentioned for the longer term.

I maintain that the options market expects volatility in the short term, which translates into distrust about AMRC’s ability to sustain recurring conversion of backlog into cash.

16x EV/EBITDA supports $27 price target

With this healthy underlying growth in the fundamentals, my valuation starts with the EV/EBITDA metric, and my hypothesis starts from an EBITDA of $215M for 2025 after this Q2.

Although the market currently values AMRC at around 15x forward EV/EBITDA, I estimate a multiple of 16x, which is the line that most closely resembles mixed profiles between EPC and owned assets.

In this way, the target EV would be about $3.76B, which after deducting about $2.37B of net debt, the equity value should be around $1.39B. If we divide this by the 52M shares, the price should be around $27 for the next 12 months.

This result leaves AMRC at a discount when it is still viewed only as an EPC company. I stress that the key to this hypothesis, for that price to be short or long, will be sustaining margins above 15% and improving the conversion of backlog into cash.

That is why my first fundamental target at 12 months is $27.

Risks: regulation, financing pressure and pending litigation

As an operator in energy projects, AMRC is exposed to different regulatory and financial risks. The new fiscal framework modifies the timelines and conditions for accessing the Investment Tax Credits, which are tax credits for investment in renewables. In addition, a schedule is being introduced that will reduce incentives for battery storage starting in 2034.

Requirements are also being tightened to encourage the use of components manufactured in the US and of Foreign Entity of Concern, meaning that suppliers should not come from “high-risk” countries such as China or Russia. In this way, failure to comply with these conditions would result in a loss of eligibility for these incentives with the subsequent impact on margins. A solar project that previously had a 10% IRR could end up falling below breakeven.

Interest rates can also be a risk factor, as they make project financing more expensive and reduce expected returns. This means that every 100 bps will reduce the project’s return by about 10%. In addition, macro factors such as a slowdown in infrastructure or renewable investment, together with supply chain risks, recent tariffs and geopolitical tensions, are additional vectors of volatility for the business.

Finally, the company is involved in several lawsuits such as the agreement with SCE for the three BESS (537 MW) for which it maintains liquidated damages of up to $89M in dispute. There is also the case of Powin, a battery supplier that is in Chapter 11, with a $27M claim from AMRC.

This is the fuel we talk about when we refer to the market’s skepticism about turning backlog into cash, and why an execution misstep could end up proving short sellers right.

Competitor Analysis: AMRC trades like an EPC, not like a utility

If we put Ameresco against its peers, with a forward EV/EBITDA of 15x, it is in line with companies like BWMN (14x) or LMB (12x), and well above GLDD or CDLR, which trade in single digits. That parity with engineering mid-caps is the signal that the market does not yet assign it a premium multiple as an asset operator.

However I do see that its fundamentals point higher than what it is currently assigned. With the $5.1B backlog, and more than 65% of adjusted EBITDA already coming from assets with recurring cash flow, and with 10–20 year contracts, I see AMRC more as a mixed model than an EPC. If it manages to consolidate margins above 15% and convert the backlog into cash on a recurring basis, it should be compared to Stantec (STN) or even small green utilities, where multiples move in the 17–19x EV/EBITDA range.

In my opinion, this gap reflects skepticism more than a fair valuation. The market pays Ameresco today as if it were a contractor with high execution risk. And this is where I see the upside potential if it delivers quarter after quarter as it has been doing.

The technical chart at a turning point

After bouncing strongly from the capitulation lows near $15 earlier this year, AMRC’s technical setup is at an interesting moment.

The price has recovered the 50-day moving average and is now pressing the long-term downtrend line that has capped rebounds since late 2021. The $25–26 zone concentrates that resistance and an important volume area, making it the ceiling that converges with the fundamental hypothesis we just outlined.

On the downside, the floor remains at $15–16, where buyers have already defended the price several times. If the price loses that level, it would return the scenario to the previous capitulation area around $12.

The bullish hypothesis comes from a clear breakout above $26, which would open the door to a test of $30, and if surpassed, would take us to the 200-day moving average near $41. That average also reminds us that the long-term trend remains bearish.

Momentum has improved, as the RSI is advancing toward the 65 zone, which reflects strength without yet entering overbought territory. In my opinion, both a sustained breakout above $26 and a subsequent confirmation are needed before declaring the long-term downtrend cycle finished.

Ameresco remains a contrarian bet with rerating potential

I maintain a Buy rating on AMRC.

The market values AMRC with EPC-type multiples, but its margins and revenue mix tell me otherwise. The backlog also reinforces this asymmetry.

The $27 per share target within the next 12 months would reflect the current discount, as well as the possibility that the market begins to value AMRC in line with the hypothesis I lay out in this article. That would also confirm the fundamental valuation I present and is consistent with the technical setup.

data.title: Nvidia: The AI Juggernaut Poised For Another Beat (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

Since I turned bullish on Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) stock in January 2021, which I admit was kind of late, it's up by over 30%, beating the S&P 500 index (SPX) (SP500) by a factor of over 3.5, and since my most recent update in June, NVDA gained ~23.6%.

In June, I was arguing that NVDA was set to add another $1 trillion to its market cap despite hitting its ATHs at the time, and so it happened.

Despite near ATHs, Nvidia's valuation is justified by reduced cyclicality and robotics potential; I see a $181 price target based on underestimated growth and sustained AI leadership.

Now, as we're approaching NVDA's Q2 print, I'd like to do a preview and figure out what the stock's prospects look like now, and whether it's still worth having a "Buy" rating on Nvidia right before its well-anticipated earnings release.

Previewing Nvidia's Q2 earnings

Back in June, I covered the main things from the firm's Q1 print, so for now, I'll just briefly mention what I think is important for the Q2 preview discussion.

In Q1, NVDA delivered double-beating results, with revenue of $44.06 billion (+69.2% YoY) and adjusted EPS of $0.81 (+32.7% YoY), which were 1.88% and 8.02% higher than initially expected by the market, respectively. In a big way, the underestimation on the analysts' end came from the $4.5 billion charge related to H20 inventory write-downs following U.S. export control restrictions on China sales, but Nvidia proved that it had enough operating leverage and other markets' demand to offset the China-related sales cuts (hence the 8%+ beat on the bottom line).

Their DC segment, which grew by 73% YoY, now consists of ~70% coming from Blackwell architecture, which is pricier than Hopper and hence should bring in higher margins in general. Indeed, adjusting for the Chinese factors' impact on the Q1 earnings, the firm's gross margins would have been close to 71.3%, which isn't the peak because we saw almost 75% in Q4, but that would be indicative of their everlasting pricing power.

I assume that the transition towards Balcwell is still going strong because the longer-term savings are going to be massive for Nvidia's clientele. Here's how one Redditor calculated that:

By switching from Hopper to Blackwell, OpenAI could save $1.43 million per evaluation cycle while benefiting from better performance and energy efficiency.

If so, I'd not see any major setbacks in NVDA's profitability, at least in the next few quarters.

The rise in inference demand, in my understanding, is what should give Nvidia a new growth vector because reasoning models require "hundreds to thousands of times more tokens per task," and it's impossible for LLM providers to compete without the interconnected systems and software that Nvidia offers. For example, their Dynamo software provides a 30x inference throughput improvement, and it's a big win for any AI firm that comes first in adopting it in its workflow.

So, the general backdrop for Nvidia remains strong, and I don't see any demand disruptions or market saturation with the firm's products, at least for now.

When it comes to Nvidia's specific Q2 numbers, we know that the management guided for $45 billion +/- 2% (+50% YoY), having excluded the $8 billion impact from China H20 sales. I think it looks a bit conservative because the hyperscalers' CAPEX spending hasn't gone anywhere, and the current Blackwell adoption suggests that with new end markets (for instance, the sovereign AI expansion initiatives), all that can easily lead to higher top-line growth figures this quarter.

The "sovereign AI story" has been incredibly strong lately, and as a prominent example, I can recall Germany's Deutsche Telekom's purchase of 10,000 GPUs [announced in June], and it's just a single European company, with likely many more to come shortly. I'd expect the Nvidia management team to be more specific on the post-Q2 earnings call regarding what they see on this front, but there's no sign visible to me suggesting that they'll sound bearish.

In general, my optimistic views seem to be shared by Wall Street analysts as they expect $46 billion in sales for Q2, which is $1 billion more compared to the management's midrange target of $45 billion, and it's even above the guided 2% deviation.

As the CFO noted on the call, the firm's GAAP and non-GAAP gross margins should be close to 71.8% and 72%, respectively, with "continued progress toward mid-70% gross margins later in the year." All else equal, it's something close to what we saw in Q3 FY2025, when Nvidia's GP came in at 72.72%. Back then, the net income margin was ~48.85%, according to Seeking Alpha, but NVDA managed to increase its profit margin even at a lower GP margin, so that 48-49% range is now looking too pessimistic. I think we can take 54-55% as a close Q2 FY2026 assumption because on a TTM basis, at 70.1% GP margin, Nvidia holds a 51.69% profit margin (logically, if they have 72% in GP margin now in Q2, the operating leverage effect can send their net margin higher than the TTM figure). Seeking Alpha data shows that the firm has been reducing its share count over the past few quarters, and the last QoQ change came in at -0.28%, so that's what I'll take as a core assumption.

With all the above calculations in mind, I get an implied EPS figure for Q2 at $1.016, which is a few cents above the current consensus for the upcoming quarter:

Revenue Q2 * Net margin / (# shares Q1 * Reduction factor) = Q2 EPS

$46,000 * 0.545 / (24734.3 * 0.9972) = $1.016

So, all else being equal, I think that if Nvidia reports at least in-line revenue this time, its existing operating leverage should be enough to beat the bottom line consensus estimate relatively easily. The history is on the bulls' side:

Nvidia's Valuation Approaching Q2 Report

Last time, I came up with a price target of $181/share, but it was in June, and since then, NVDA touched that price mark before correcting to ~$178/share at the time of this writing.

I was basing my PT on FY2027 EPS, assuming a 30x in earnings multiple and a slight 2-3% premium on the consensus estimates at the time.

Looking at the history of NVDA's EPS beats, I think it's not that hard to assume a 2-3% premium on the current consensus estimates in the next 2 years (at least). So, if my math is correct, FY2027 EPS should come in at ~$6.04 (instead of $5.74 priced in today). At 30x earnings, NVDA becomes a $181/share stock (+26% upside).

But I'm not so sure now that Nvidia should be targeting a 30x earnings multiple by the end of fiscal 2027 because that would give them a forward PEG ratio of just 0.79x (at the consensus FY2027 EPS growth rate). Interestingly, Nvidia has no negative annual YoY growth rates expected in the consensus over the next decade, despite the company's cyclical nature.

If so, if the consensus is right about the sustainability of the earnings growth, albeit deteriorating over time, the stock itself should hold a premium in its valuation.

I think we might start to see 1.36x PEG priced on FY2028 earlier than that. For FY2027, I think it can be at least 1x. So, if we still apply the same 2-3% on top of the current FY2027 EPS consensus, and we derive the implied PEG, we can get an updated upside potential by comparing the target PEG with what we get.

(FY2027 EPS * Premium / FY2026 EPS) -1 = FY2027 EPS growth rate

Current price / Updated FY2027 EPS / new FY2027 EPS growth rate = New FY2027 PEG

(Target PEG / New FY2027 PEG) -1 = Upside %

Let's plug in the numbers now:

($5.99 * 1.025 / $4.35) -1 = +41.1%

($178.32 / ($5.99 * 1.025)) / 41.14 = ~0.71x

(1.00 / 0.71) -1 = ~0.42%

So, my new valuation model, which I updated relying on PEGs rather than on P/Es, says that NVDA may gain another 42% in market cap in the next 12 months or so.

Risks Of Staying Bullish

There are some points that all Nvidia bulls should be critical about. For example, the continued rise in Advanced Micro Devices' (AMD) role in the GPU market. This Nvidia's peer holds 4-5% in the GPU market, according to some sources, and it can be much higher than that in the next few years as they keep rolling out new competitive offerings. So, if Nvidia starts to lose big on its dominance, it can start a pricing war, which can lead to lower margins and hence less valuation premium over time.

Another risk is my set of assumptions regarding the upcoming Q2. I do take a bold input for my Q2 EPS calculation that Nvidia will reach 54-55% net margin for the quarter, and while it's doable for them, the core of this forecast is a bit risky in itself. We can't know the Q2 share count just yet, and we don't know whether the analysts' $46 billion Q2 sales target, which is $1 billion more than the management guided for back in May, will be there. So, if they don't report at least in line with the top line consensus estimate, and if the share count doesn't go down on a QoQ basis by at least 25-30 bps, my EPS calculation will be off, and so the firm can miss on the bottom line this time.

The Bottom Line

Please be aware that earnings dates are mostly accompanied by pretty high volatilities and moves, which aren't always to the upside. For Nvidia's upcoming Q2, I think the odds are that we'll see a nice bottom-line beat again, and it'll catalyze the stock to the upside eventually. The valuation multiples have some room for expansion on the FWD basis, although it might seem like there's nothing left on a TTM basis.

I decided to reiterate my "Buy" on NVDA right before its Q2 print.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: IBM: The Retracement An Opportunity As IBM Has Transformed Its Business
data.description:
I’ve been long International Business Machines (NYSE:IBM) since 2018 and I believe the recent retracement in its share price is an opportunity for investors. Over the past several years IBM has undergone a drastic transformation as it’s shed a large portion of its legacy hardware and services operations and focused on hybrid cloud, artificial intelligence (AI), and software solutions. IBM faced declining revenue in its legacy businesses and instead of trying to squeeze an additional few percentage points of margin out of declining businesses they made the hard decision and pivoted their focus to the segments that would lead technology in the future. IBM is capitalizing on emerging technologies such as generative AI, and quantum computing by making many strategic acquisitions in these areas while divesting non-core assets. I believe that under Arvind Krishna’s guidance IBM has positioned itself as a hybrid cloud provider as they have shifted their focus from directly competing with AWS, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud in public cloud infrastructure to combining on-premises and public cloud environments. IBM’s focus toward the technologies of tomorrow have allowed them to generate increased levels of profitability with higher margins since 2019 and I am still bullish about the investment opportunity as IBM is trading at less than 20 times 2027 earnings. Unless the progression in technology stops, I think IBM will continue to benefit from rapid AI adoption.

Following up on my previous article about IBM

Back in April I wrote an article about IBM (can be read here) where I discussed why acquiring Hakkoda enhanced its transformation to emerging technologies from it’s legacy businesses. IBM’s financials indicated that it’s pivoted into a leader in cloud, consulting, and AI. I outlined why IBM’s focus on data transformation services would work well for them especially since their Hakkoda acquisition provided them with the ability to migrate, modernize, and help clients monetize their data. IBM’s ability to offer advanced AI solutions and enhance its client's competitive edge would result in more revenue being generated by IBM’s business segments. Q2 earnings showcased how IBM continues to grow from a pivot on its strategy and I am bullish on its ability to continue growing and delivering returns for shareholders.

Risks to investing in IBM

While I am bullish on IBM there are risks that investors should consider and everyone should do their own due diligence. The S&P 500 is at its all-time highs as it closed at 6,466.91 on Friday 8/22, yet shares of IBM have retraced -18.26% since reaching all-time highs in June. IBM generated $16.98 billion in revenue during Q2 which was $385.47 million above the consensus estimates and beat on EPS by $0.04 but it’s share price has dropped -14.16% since Q2 earnings were released. IBM may have reached the point where a lot of the value has been captured, and it may be hard for shares to outperform the market going forward. IBM still faces intense competition in AI and cloud from hyperscalers and if they are unable to innovate in areas like generative AI or hybrid cloud their market share could face significant pressure. IBM could face challenges if AI adoption from enterprise clients doesn’t expand and if its AI initiatives or other growth areas don’t generate increased levels of revenue IBM’s turnaround could be put in jeopardy. Everyone should do their own research as IBM has tremendous competition across the areas it competes in.

IBM continues to showcase it’s ability to pivot from it’s legacy businesses

Over the past several years IBM has acquired Octo for federal digital transformation, Apptio for IT financial management, Hakkoda for data consulting, and HashiCorp for infrastructure automation which have helped position IBM for a digitalized future. In 2019 IBM generated $56.5 billion in revenue, $31.53 billion in gross profit, $13,6 billion in EBITDA, and $7.54 billion in operating income. Since then IBM’s revenue has increased by $6.84 billion (12.10%) to $63.34 billion while increasing profitability. IBM’s gross profit increased $5.33 billion (16.92%) to $36.87 billion while its gross profit margin expanded 2.4% to 58.21%. IBM increased it’s EBITDA by $585 million (4.3%) and its operating income by $.34 billion (44.24%) over this period. IBM’s operating margin increased 3.83% to 17.17% from 2019 through the TTM as it’s become a more profitable company. In Q2 2025 IBM’s financial trends continued as their revenue increased by 7.76% YoY ($1.21 billion) to $16.81 billion while it’s gross profit expanded to 11.47% ($1.03 billion) YoY and its operating income grew 32.17% ($757 million YoY) to $3.11 billion. IBM continues to showcase its ability to grow its top and bottom line after divesting part of it’s legacy businesses and focusing on high growth opportunities.

I believe that IBM delivered solid operational results which exceeded the streets expectations. The top and bottom line beat for IBM was driven by its software and infrastructure segments. IBM’s strategy of focusing on hybrid cloud and AI continues to differentiate it in the market as their able to help clients deploy and scale their AI offerings. IBM’s generative AI book of business accelerated to over $7.5 billion which showcases the market's demand for IBM's AI offerings. IBM's operational highlights were significantly strengthened by its software and infrastructure businesses, critical advancements in AI and strategic acquisitions. The software segment generated $7.4 billion in revenue which was an increase of 8% YoY. Within IBM’s software segment its hybrid cloud increased 14% YoY while automation increased by 14% YoY also. IBM is benefiting from the integration of HashiCorp as its data revenue grew 7% YoY and all its progress has led to IBM’s software division generating $22.7 billion in ARR.

IBM also grew its revenue in the infrastructure segment as it’s revenue increased 11% YoY to $4.1 billion. This was fueled by a 70% increase in IBM Z revenue which highlighted the successful early adoption of the new z17 mainframe program. The z17 incorporates the new Telum II processor, capable of over 450 billion AI inference operations per day with millisecond latency. This allows direct AI model execution on transactional workloads. IBM’s upcoming Spyre Accelerator will further integrate watsonx Code Assistant for Z and watsonx Assistant for Z natively on z17 which they believe will enhance IBM’s AI capabilities across the infrastructure segment. IBM’s consulting segment generated $5.3 billion in revenue which was flat YoY and even though there was no YoY growth its generative AI book of business contributed over $1 billion of revenue. I believe that IBM’s product innovations including watsonx Orchestrate for custom AI agent building and watsonx data for unstructured data access will help to drive additional engagement across it’s client base. IBM’s strategic partnerships with the hyperscalers should help expand the integration of watsonx into core business workflows which should allow IBM’s consulting services to generate additional as companies focus on implementing AI offerings into their environment.

There could still be value to be unlocked in shares of IBM after the recent retracement

When I go through IBM’s cash flow statement I believe there could still be value to be unlocked as the share price retracement has created an opportunity. In the TTM IBM has generated $12.4 billion in free cash flow (FCF) and rewarded shareholders with $738 million worth of buybacks conducted while IBM allocated $6.2 billion toward dividend payments. IBM is allocating 57% of it’s FCF to its capital allocation program and there is more than enough room for IBM to increase buybacks or continue providing annualized dividend increases. With a market cap of $225.51 billion IBM is trading at 18.43 times it’s FCF which looks inexpensive compared to the hyperscaler’s. When I compare IBM to the Magnificent Seven the next cheapest valuation is Apple (AAPL) as it trades at 34.32 times it’s FCF while Meta Platforms (META) and Alphabet (GOOGL) trade at 36.25 times and 37.43 times FCF. I think that there is room for IBM’s valuation to expand as it should be trading for closer to 25x FCF.

Next, I looked at IBM for a forward earnings perspective and I believe that the market is discounting their ability to increase EPS over the next several years. IBM has beat the consensus EPS estimates since Q1 2023 by an average of $0.13. Based on IBMs revenue growth and their track record of exceeding EPS expectations I believe that the forward EPS estimates of $11.85 in 2026 and $12.52 in 2027 is a bit light. At the current levels IBM is trading at 21.81 times 2025 earnings with 12.79% of expected EPS growth over the next 2 years placing their 2027 valuation at 19.34 times 2027 earnings. The Coca-Cola Company (KO) is expected to grow their EPS by 15.77% over this period and I think there is more growth on the horizon for IBM as they gain a significant amount of business from implementing AI across its enterprise clients. I believe that IBM will earn closer to $13.32 of EPS in 2027 which would mean it’s trading at 18.17 times 2027 earnings with 20% of EPS growth over the next 2 years. If that occurs, I think it could take shares well past the $300 level.

Conclusion

IBM is no longer the undervalued dividend play exceeding a 5% yield, but the recent retracement has created an opportunity for capital appreciation. Shares of IBM have declined by just over -18% from their recent highs and Q2 earnings were strong despite a negative narrative being built around the lack of growth in the consulting segment. IBM is seeing strength across its AI and hybrid cloud offerings and there could be a larger opportunity over the next several years as a larger portion of IT budgets are focused on corporate AI strategies. I think that investors are getting a solid dividend yield of 2.78% with 25 years of dividend growth at the current share price while they wait for shares to rebound. Many investors wrote IBM off as a dying technology company but over the past several years IBM has proved people wrong by shifting its focus to growth areas while divesting legacy businesses. I think the market is underestimating IBM’s forward growth potential and making too big of a deal about its consulting segment. I believe that shares of IBM are attractive at these levels and there is still significant value to be unlocked.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: Here's A Post-Powell Trend You Can Bank On
data.description:
By Mike Larson

We often see wild trading action on big news days. Sometimes, we’ll get huge moves after the headlines hit - but they fail to stick for a single session, much less a week, a month, or a quarter. But I have one “Post-Powell” trend I think you can bank on!

Here’s the MoneyShow Chart of the Day. It shows the 2-year/10-year Treasury yield spread going back five years. As the name suggests, this is the difference between the yield on the 2-year Treasury Note and the yield on the 10-year Treasury Note. A positive number means the latter is higher than the former, while a negative number means the opposite is true.

2/10 Treasury Yield Spread

(Data by YCharts)

You can see that after a lengthy stint in negative territory, the 2/10 spread grew to 0.54%, or 54 basis points, as of last Thursday. That’s what you typically see when economic worries subside. It’s also what happens when bond markets grow more concerned about future inflation risk.

That second point is key. Last Friday, Federal Reserve Chairman Jay Powell gave an incredibly important speech in Jackson Hole, Wyoming. He essentially confirmed that the Fed will cut rates at its meeting that ends Sept. 17.

But what happened? Short-term yields fell much more than long-term yields. The 2-year yield dropped 10 basis points... while the 10-year yield fell only 7 bps. Even further out on the curve, the yield on the 30-year Treasury Bond dipped just 4 bps.

I’ve been analyzing the bond market and Fed policymaking closely for a quarter century. The message here is simple. Bond investors are pricing in the risk that more easy money now will fuel greater inflation risk later. The political pressure being brought to bear on the Fed is amplifying those concerns.

In short, I think the “steepening yield curve” trend is here to stay. You can trade it using interest rate futures and futures options, if that’s your thing. Or... you could just buy bank stocks!

As a general rule, banks benefit from a steeper curve because they borrow short and lend long. The greater the spread between short-term and long-term yields, the fatter their profit margins from core lending operations.

The Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLF) was up 2.1% in the month leading up to Powell Day, while the SPDR S&P Bank ETF (KBE) was up 3.8%. The Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK)? Up just 0.4%. Then, on Friday, the KBE rose 4%, more than double the daily gain on the XLK.

That outperformance might just be a trend you can... bank on.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CrowdStrike: Down 20% In 2 Months, Is It A Buy Ahead Of Earnings?
data.description:
Introduction

Cybersecurity titan CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:CRWD) is all set to report its Q2 FY2026 earnings on Wednesday, 27th August 2025, in the after-hours session. Now, despite responding to its rival Palo Alto Networks' (PANW) entry (via the impending acquisition of CyberArk (CYBR)) into the Identity Security market with the unveiling of Falcon Next-Gen Identity Security solution in mid-August, CrowdStrike's stock has failed to regain momentum in a resilient market tape - underperforming benchmarks - and is sitting ~20% off its recent highs heading into the quarterly print.

In today's note, we will preview CrowdStrike's Q2 FY2026 report and re-run CRWD stock through TQI's valuation model to evaluate this pre-ER dip buying opportunity. Let's go!

What Is The Earnings Forecast For CrowdStrike?

In their Q1 FY2026 earnings report, CrowdStrike's management guided for Q2 revenues to be in the range of $1.145-$1.151B [+19% y/y], with non-GAAP EPS projected to land at $0.82-0.84 per share [-20% y/y].

And, heading into the print, both top and bottom line consensus analyst estimates are sitting pretty much in line with the company's guidance, with Wall Street only slightly more optimistic on sales, projecting CrowdStrike to deliver sales at the top end of management's guided range:

That said, revision trends for CrowdStrike's Q2 revenue estimates have mainly been tilted to the downside, with "36 Down" vs. "3 Up" Revisions.

Clearly, sell-side analysts have been adjusting their estimates for Q2 revenue lower in recent weeks, i.e., becoming less optimistic.

Will CrowdStrike Deliver An Earnings Beat?

Now, CrowdStrike's management has a history of underpromising and overdelivering, so I wouldn't bet against George Kurtz & Co. delivering a "beat and raise" quarter; however, CrowdStrike did miss revenue expectations last quarter, albeit by just around $2M.

Also, despite highlighting strong Falcon deal momentum and a robust pipeline for the back half of the year, CrowdStrike's executive leadership team failed to lift its full-year FY2026 revenue guidance, primarily due to the negative impact of CrowdStrike's Customer Choice Program [$11M in Q1 FY2026, est. $10-15M in each of the remaining quarters of FY2026]:

We believe cybersecurity remains mission-critical in today's AI-accelerated threat environment. We continue to see strong demand for the Falcon platform, growing momentum with Falcon Flex, including reflexes and a robust and growing pipeline building for the second half of FY '26. While we do not guide to net new ARR, our Q2 assumptions include the sequential net new ARR growth rate to be at least double over what we saw from Q1 to Q2 in the prior fiscal year. I'd like to take a minute to discuss the near-term relationship between ARR and subscription revenue in FY '26. As we previously discussed, our successful CCP program that concluded in Q4 of FY '25 provided customers the onetime ability to choose more product, more time or both, which results in an impact to subscription revenue. In addition, a limited special partner program related to CCP's success also has an amortization impact on subscription revenue. As a result of these CCP-related programs, we expect to see a temporary near-term separation between ARR and subscription revenue recognition, which was reflected in our revenue guidance. This amounted to approximately $11 million in Q1. We expect the impact to be in the range of $10 million to $15 million in each remaining quarter of this fiscal year, subsiding in Q4.

- Burt Podbere, CrowdStrike's Q1 FY2026 Earnings Call (Transcript)

Furthermore, CrowdStrike's leadership expects ongoing restructuring efforts [eliminating 5% of CRWD's global workforce] and the July 19 outage and related expenses to hurt the company's cash flow production in the near term.

Moving to the strategic realignment plan we announced in early May, we continuously look for optimizations and efficiencies across the business. We identified opportunities to reallocate and focus investment in, one, platform growth areas of cloud, identity, exposure management, AI and Next-Gen SIEM as well as platform resilience; two, AI to accelerate our internal execution and efficiency; and three, go to market and customer success as we scale.

The timing of executing the realignment in early May was focused on minimizing in-quarter business disruption while maximizing in- quarter financial benefit. We expect the full benefit of the realignment to add at least 1% to next year's non-GAAP operating margin from our previously discussed target, increasing our target in FY '27 to at least 24%. Additionally, we now anticipate an FY '27 improved free cash flow margin of more than 30%.

Moving to cash. We expect to incur Q2 cash charges of approximately $26 million in connection with the aforementioned strategic plan. Additionally, we expect Q2 free cash flow to be impacted by approximately $29 million for outage and related expenses. As I just mentioned, we now anticipate an FY '27 free cash flow margin of more than 30%.

CrowdStrike's Valuation Problem And Troublesome Technical Setup

Given CrowdStrike's best-of-breed cybersecurity platform, this blip in financial performance is very likely to be ephemeral. However, with CRWD stock priced for perfection at >25x P/S and >100x P/FCF, Mr. Market may have no patience for a rebound in business momentum, despite CrowdStrike's new $1B buyback program at work.

In my view, CrowdStrike has little to no margin for error this quarter, and any disappointment in the Q2 FY2026 report could easily accelerate CRWD's ongoing slide, with a potential breakdown of the 40-week moving average at ~$410 likely to trigger a downleg to the long-term support trendline, which currently lies within the 100-week MA [$335] and 200-week MA [$250] moving average support levels.

Now, on the flip side, a robust quarter and uplifted forward guidance could re-energize CRWD stock back up to all-time highs, especially if the broad market resilience holds.

As we know, in the short run, stocks can fluctuate significantly, up and down. However, in the long run, they tend to follow the intrinsic value of the underlying business. Let us now re-run CRWD through TQI's valuation model to reach an informed decision.

CrowdStrike's Fair Value And Expected Return

For today's valuation exercise, I am updating our CRWD model's revenue base to $4.8B [FY2026E revenue]. Furthermore, despite some near-term business uncertainty, CrowdStrike's path to $10B ARR is quite clear. Given its product leadership and massive [+growing] TAM ($116B in CY2025 -> $250B in CY2029), I continue to assume 25% [above-consensus] CAGR top-line growth for CrowdStrike.

On the margin front, CrowdStrike's long-term operating model calls for a 34-38% FCF margin, and so, despite expected near-term cash flow pressures, I think our optimized FCF margin assumption of 37.5% is reasonable.

All other model assumptions are relatively straightforward, but please feel free to share any questions in the comments section below.

Here's my updated valuation model for CrowdStrike:

According to TQI's valuation model, CrowdStrike is worth ~$269.5 per share ($69B in market cap) [up from our previous assessment of $57B]. However, with CRWD stock currently trading at ~$421 per share, I see -36% downside to fair value.

Assuming a premium exit multiple of 25x P/FCF, I can see CRWD trading at $622 five years from now, which implies a 5-year CAGR return of ~8%.

While CrowdStrike's expected 5-year CAGR return appears to be in line with long-term stock market returns of 8-10%, it falls well short of TQI's investment hurdle rate of 15%. Hence, CrowdStrike is a "Hold" under our valuation process.

Concluding Thoughts

In the digital age, cybersecurity software is essential infrastructure for all businesses, regardless of their sector or industry. This ongoing growth trend is expected to continue for several more years. Although CrowdStrike's revenue growth rate has slowed significantly in recent quarters, CrowdStrike Falcon remains a leading cybersecurity platform that should continue to grow at a healthy clip in the upcoming years and potentially decades.

Now, while I adore CrowdStrike as a business (reflected in optimistic model assumptions), CRWD's valuation is still too rich, and consequently, the expected forward return is sub-optimal.

Hence, despite CrowdStrike's ~20% pre-ER dip, I continue to remain neutral on CRWD stock.

Key Takeaway: I rate CrowdStrike a "Hold/Neutral" in the $400s.

Thank you for reading, and happy investing! Please share any questions, thoughts, and/or concerns in the comments section below or DM me.

data.title: Youdao: Tepid Revenue Growth Will Ultimately Limit Earnings Growth
data.description:
Youdao (NYSE:DAO), a developer and supplier of technologies needed to provide online learning content, applications and solutions, released its most recent earnings report on August 14, which shows how DAO continues to make significant progress in a key area that is in urgent need of improvement. However, DAO needs to show more because if current trends hold, particularly as it relates to revenue growth and gross margin, long DAO is unlikely to be a winning play. Why will be covered next.

DAO has gone sideways

A previous article from last March mentioned how DAO had followed a trendline on the way higher, a rally that was assisted by enthusiasm surrounding artificial intelligence or AI, but this rally came to an end after the stock fell below the trendline, in large part because of an FY2025 outlook that called for only modest growth, which seemed contrary to the promise of AI as an enabler of faster growth.

The breakdown in the stock suggested more downside ahead as DAO searched for support to halt the decline, which the article speculated could be at one of the Fibonacci retracement levels. This was deemed to be most likely the 61.8% retracement level, or $6.28, a price level well below where DAO was at the time. Accordingly, I decided to rate DAO a hold in the article.

The chart above showed how the article hit the nail on the head on in terms of picking the likely end point for the decline in the stock because the stock proceeded to hit a low of $6.30 on April 9, before reversing course. This is just $0.02 above $6.28, or where the previous article suggested the stock might be able to find support and stem the decline in the stock.

Recall how the stock hit a 52-weeks high of $11.82 in February 2025, which was the culmination of a rally that started with a low of $2.86 in August 2024. The 61.8% Fibonacci retracement of $2.86 to $11.82 is $6.28, which suggests DAO has found the support needed to not only put an end to the decline in the stock, but also from where it can move higher.

However, while there was a substantial bounce following the April low, DAO has moved mostly sideways in recent weeks. Nothing thus far has been able to alter the inability to move ahead, which includes the release of the most recent or Q2 FY2025 report. The report was followed by a spike in the stock, but it surrendered most of the post-earnings gains almost immediately. DAO looks to be stuck in the $8-10 region for now.

DAO is making progress according to the latest updates

The stock has not done all that much in recent weeks, but DAO is still worth about three times as much compared to a year ago with a market cap of $1,086M. This would not have happened without the rally in the stock, which in turn would not have been possible without the current investor enthusiasm for AI and ways to play the boom in AI. DAO has been focusing on the application of AI, which turned DAO into the type of company people are very interested in as an AI play.

On the other hand, DAO also has what could be seen by many as a red flag in the sense that it has more liabilities than assets, which means DAO is insolvent on a balance sheet basis. This is not something that cannot be fixed, nor does it have to lead to something shareholders will come to regret, but it does make DAO a risky investment since it is under a certain degree of financial stress.

This is why any gain in the income statement is important, because it provides a pathway for DAO to get out of the undesirable position the balance sheet finds itself in, something AI can assist with. As mentioned earlier, the rally from earlier in the year got derailed after FY2025 guidance came in underwhelming in terms of the growth outlook, but DAO has still been making significant improvements to the income statement, including in the latest report.

In the Q2 FY2025 report, DAO reported a non-GAAP profit of RMB0.10 or $0.01 per ADS on revenue of RMB1,417.5M or $197.9M. In comparison, DAO reported a non-GAAP loss of RMB0.82 per ADS on revenue of RMB1,321.7M in Q2 FY2024. In terms of GAAP, DAO reported a loss of $0.02 per ADS, but this was also much better than the loss of $0.12 per ADS a year ago.

DAO was able to greatly improve the bottom line even though the top line grew modestly, mostly because of a decrease in operating expenses like sales and marketing, and research and development to a lesser extent. DAO finished the quarter with total assets of RMB1,841.1M or $257.0M, but this was more than offset by total liabilities of RMB3,851.4M or $537.6M on the balance sheet, which is a slight improvement compared to last year.

Is DAO improving fast enough?

DAO earned $0.10 in terms of GAAP and 0.11 in terms of non-GAAP per ADS in FY2024 and it has earned another $0.07 and $0.11, respectively, in H1 FY2025. Assuming DAO earns another $0.11 in the second half of the year, then my estimate for FY2025 non-GAAP earnings is to end up at $0.22 per ADS. The stock closed at $9.08 per ADS on August 22, which implies DAO is trading at 41.3x FY2025 earnings, using the FY2025 estimate of $0.22 per ADS.

According to the methodology favored by Peter Lynch, DAO therefore needs to grow EPADS at a CAGR of 41.3% in the next 3-5 years to be considered fairly valued at current valuations relative to growth. In order to hit this rate of growth, FY2028 EPADS would need to hit $0.63. On the surface, adding another $0.41 to FY2025 EPADS of $0.22 in three years looks like something that is feasible. DAO after all is on track to more than double earnings this year and if it can maintain this pace in the next several years, then $0.63 at the end of FY2028 is not only achievable, but something that can be surpassed.

However, there is reason to believe that the above may not pan out. This is because a major deceleration in the rate of earnings improvement in the coming years is not out of the question. Most of the earnings improvement in the latest quarter, for instance, was due to a decrease in operating expenses, but this is not sustainable in the long run.

Operating expenses decreased by RMB128.7M YoY in Q2 FY2025. In comparison non-GAAP net income attributable to shareholders flipped from a loss of RMB95.97M in Q2 FY2024 to a profit of RMB12.52M in Q2 FY2025, or almost as much as what operating expenses fell by. In other words, if not for the decrease in expenses on sales, marketing, research and development, the bottom line would have seen no improvement.

The issue here is that while there is growth in the top line, it is not growing fast enough to enable sufficient growth in the bottom line, which is why DAO needs to resort to other areas to make up for it, including by spending less on certain operating expenses. It does not help that gross margins have steadily decreased for the last two years.

However, while this approach can improve earnings in the short term, it is unlikely to be a viable method over the long run. If earnings growth is to be sustainable, at a level that corresponds to valuations, faster revenue growth is needed, more than what DAO has shown recently. Revenue growth has actually gradually slowed down in recent years.

Revenue only grew by 2% in the last completed fiscal or FY2024 after growing by 4% the year before. In the first half of the current fiscal, revenue was almost flat with RMB2,715.8M in H1 FY2025, a little better than the RMB2,713.6M in H1 FY2024. It is difficult to see how earnings can keep improving if this pattern holds. If the income statement stalls and it does not improve, DAO will be hard-pressed to eliminate the $280.6M gap between liabilities and assets in order to get rid of the label of being insolvent.

There is therefore reason to doubt the target of a CAGR of 41.3% for earnings growth will be achieved by DAO. If growth comes in significantly below, which will happen if revenue growth retains its current pace, then DAO could be overvalued relative to expected growth and therefore not worth buying at current levels.

Investor takeaways

DAO has done quite well in the last 12 months. Market cap, for instance, has tripled thanks to a huge rally in the stock. The stock did experience a correction after FY2025 guidance was not as strong as some hoped for, but DAO was able to reverse the decline to retain most gains after finding support at a key level.

The income statement has shown major gains, including in the latest report. The Q2 FY2025 report, for example, included income from operation that was not in the red for the first time in a second quarter. The Q2 FY2025 report showed that YTD non-GAAP EPADS has already equaled the $0.11 earned in all of last year and that is with one half to go in the current fiscal.

The public’s response to various AI-enabled products from DAO seems to be positive. Youdao Lingshi has gotten a good response, and so too has the Youdao Pen, which managed to hit top rankings in term of sales on e-commerce sites. Online ad services is also making good progress, including by incorporating AI.

However, the stock has stalled more recently. This is probably warranted after the huge run-up in price it had, but there is also reason to question whether DAO has what it takes to move the stock higher in the long run. There is reason to doubt whether DAO will be able to keep improving the income statement, which is needed to fix the balance sheet.

While DAO has improved earnings, there are a number of worrying signs that point to a deceleration in growth ahead to the point that it will come in at a rate that is below that needed to justify current valuations. Revenue is growing, but too slowly and gross margins keep ticking down. DAO has managed to offset this with cutting costs, but this cannot substitute for a lack of revenue growth in the long run. At this time, if current trends holds, DOA looks to be heading for a major slowdown in earnings growth.

DAO has more liabilities than it has assets and to get out of this situation DAO needs to improve the income statement, which used to come with pretty hefty losses. DAO has made significant progress in this regard, but its ability to keep making progress is in doubt as long as revenue and margins are going in the opposite direction of where they need to go. It is not likely to happen in the short term, but it will happen eventually as things stand.

I am thus neutral on DAO with everything above in mind. If long DAO is to pay off in the long run, it needs to accompany the current improvement in earnings with faster revenue growth to be sustainable, in addition to a reversal in the decline in margins, or at least a stabilization. As long as this has yet to happen, DAO is not going to become solvent.

data.title: PayPal: Weak TPV Trends Push Growth Recovery Out To 2026
data.description:
No Inflection in the Near Term

PayPal's (NASDAQ:PYPL)'s stock has been under pressure, down nearly 20% YTD despite the overall market rally. This signals that the company's fundamentals have deteriorated. At first glance, we saw PYPL's volume per transaction and customer accounts showed improvements, but Total Payment Volume (TPV) growth has failed to turn around. This can largely impact its top-line growth. The 2Q TPV was largely affected by weaker Branded checkout, driven by tariffs impact. However, it's encouraging to see margin recovery in 2Q, but the 3Q outlook suggests a margin contraction. I believe growth reacceleration remains a key catalyst to push the stock higher, but the timing is still uncertain.

In my last 3Q FY2024 analysis, I downgraded PYPL from Buy to Hold, expecting a growth slowdown ahead. Since then, the stock has dropped 20%, compared to an 8% gain for the S&P 500. We saw a major growth slowdown in 1Q FY2025, with revenue growing only 1.2% YoY, down from 9.4% YoY in 1Q FY2024. Despite PYPL beating both revenue and non-GAAP EPS in 2Q FY2025, I still don't see growth inflection until CY2026 (assuming no major economic slowdown or recession), given weak pricing power from merchant pressure and slow adoption of new checkout products. That said, I maintain a Hold rating on PYPL, though it's trading at a low valuation.

Total Payment Volume Remains Weak

PYPL's transaction revenue (90% of total revenue) is calculated as TPV multiplied by its transaction take rate. Since take rates normally come down due to industry competition, we need to see a strong transaction revenue growth, supported by a growth reacceleration in TPV.

Let's look at this chart. After showing a modest pickup in FY2023, TPV has continued to slow. In 2Q FY2025, TPV grew 6.4% YoY (only 5% on a constant currency), which translated into 4% YoY transaction revenue growth, down from 9.1% YoY a year ago.

During the 2Q FY2025 earnings call, management noted that weak TPV growth was largely driven by a flat 5% growth in Branded checkout (high margin), weighed down by Asia tariffs. In addition, Payment Service Provider (PSP) which includes low margin unbranded checkout, grew only 2% YoY, in-line with 1Q FY2025. However, they expect Braintree (part of PSP)'s TPV to reach a major inflection in 2H FY2025. This is a big deal as PSP counts for a large part of TPV.

On the positive side, Venmo remains a bright spot, with TPV growth accelerating to 12%, the highest in 3 years. Buy Now, Pay Later (BNPL) also maintained strong growth momentum with over 20% TPV growth. Despite all these achievements, these segments remain a small part of PYPL's overall revenue

FY2025 Guidance Still Suggests Growth Slowdown

Looking forward, PYPL's 3Q FY2025 outlook does not appear to be exciting. Transaction margin dollars are expected to grow at 4% YoY at the midpoint, showing sequential slowdown from 6.5% YoY in 2Q, and from 8.5% YoY a year ago. FY2025 transaction margin dollars are expected to grow 4.5% YoY at the midpoint, which also indicates a deceleration from 7% YoY in FY2024.

On the earnings call, management guided to 3Q FY2025 revenue growth "at the lower end of mid-single digits or approximately 4%". This suggests that weak growth momentum continues, driven by muted TPV growth. While the company did raise its FY2025 guidance, I believe 2025 is a transition year for PYPL, with potential reacceleration more likely in 2026 as checkout monetization scales globally and PSP TPV rebounds. However, given the ongoing economic slowdown, PYPL's recovery could be delayed further if consumer spending weakens meaningfully.

A Big Jump in Operating Cost in 3Q FY2025

Margins are expanding on a year-over-year basis. In 2Q, non-GAAP operating margin was 19.8%, up from 18.5% a year ago. However, management guided to "high single-digit non-transaction operating expense growth" in 3Q FY2025, showing a significant increase compared to the low single digit trend earlier. This will likely pressure 3Q operating margin and lower EPS growth.

As 3Q guidance shows, the midpoint of EPS suggests flat YoY growth. Although full year 2025 EPS guidance points to 6%-10% growth, this implies a slowdown in 2H FY2025, given nearly 20% YoY growth in 1H.

Falling Back into a Value Trap?

As shown in the chart, PYPL's P/E TTM has fallen below Visa (V) and Mastercard (MA) since early 2023, and the gap continues to widen. While V and MA are not direct competitors, we can see that the market gives them much higher valuations, because they could deliver growth accelerations. By contrast, PYPL's revenue continues to decelerate.

In payments and checkout, PYPL's more direct competitors are Apple Pay, Google Pay, Stripe, and Block (XYZ). Some of them have gained more consumer attention in recent years, limiting PYPL's market share expansion. Particularly, Block's Cash App is a major competitor to Venmo, and has been gaining more user growth.

That said, PYPL remains a show-me story given its near-term growth challenge, which justifies a lower valuation. As a result, I maintain a neutral view on the stock, as the risk of a potential value trap could persist if the company continues to delay its growth inflection.

The Bottom Line

While PYPL's valuation is low, trading at 13x non-GAAP P/E fwd, the continued growth slowdown in TPV and transaction revenue suggests that market share loss and yet no near-term growth inflection from next-gen products.

Looking back to 2021, PYPL traded at +50x P/E, as investors priced in a premium valuation with sustained high growth. This could significantly bring down the multiple. However, with growth sharply slowing in recent years, the stock repriced lower, not because of revenue or earnings declines, but because investors no longer expect that premium growth profile.

PYPL's Branded checkout and PSP still looked weak in 2Q FY2025 and 3Q guidance implying flat non-GAAP EPS, I believe the growth story remains on hold. Even though a potential rebound could come in 2H FY2025, it's still too early to tell. Therefore, I maintain a Hold on PYPL due to the lack of near-term catalysts, though the stock's long-term downside risk seems limited.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Contradictory Message Of Chair Powell's Jackson Hole Address
data.description:
Market Interprets Powell's Remarks As Dovish

There was much anticipation in the financial markets this past week regarding Fed Chair Powell's opening remarks at the Kansas City Fed's Jackson Hole Economic Symposium.

I turned on his speech at 10:00 AM Friday, and immediately saw the markets had a very positive reaction. Before Chair Powell even uttered a word, the Dow Jones Industrial Average was up 700 points and the yield on the 2-year Treasury Note yield had fallen by 10 basis points. This was based on the Fed's simultaneous on-line release of Chair Powell's remarks.

By the time he started speaking shortly after 10:00 AM, the Bloomberg Channel had already posted a headline at the bottom of the screen "Fed to cut rates in September" and the markets just continued to climb.

The key phrase came about one third of the way through Chair Powell's talk when he said "with policy in restrictive territory, the baseline outlook and the shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting our policy stance."

This was no where near as definitive a declaration as last year's "A time has come for policy to adjust", but the market liked it more. Last year the Dow jumped 462 points, or 1.1% on Powell's comments, while this year the gain for the day was 846 points, or 1.9%.

The Dow surged to a record intraday high and closed at 45,632, a 10.8% gain y/y.

Powell's View Of Economic Conditions And Economic Outlook

Chair Powell highlighted how this year the economy faces many new challenges, from significantly higher tariffs, to a tighter immigration policy, and to fiscal policy changes in taxes and spending. Together these changes will have important implications for economic growth, although there is significant uncertainty about impact they will have on the economy.

On the labor front, Chair Powell addressed the large downward revisions to payroll job growth in July, which now show an average of only 35,000 new jobs for the past three months, which is down from the 168,000 new jobs per month for all of 2024. The unemployment rate, which edged up to 4.2%, still stands at a historically low rate and has been in a narrow range for the past year.

Overall, Powell felt that the labor market was in balance, but it is because of the unusual situation of slower job growth offset by fewer workers due to the new immigration policies, which suggests that the downside risks to employment are rising. He said "if those risks materialize, they can do so quickly in the form of sharply higher layoffs and rising unemployment."

As for inflation, Chair Powell acknowledged that higher tariffs are beginning to push up prices. He estimated that the soon to be released Core PCE, the Fed's preferred method of measuring inflation, ticked up to 2.9%, higher than it was a year ago.

The impact of tariffs on consumer prices is now "clearly visible," and the Fed expects that tariffs will continue to "accumulate over coming months, with high uncertainty about timing and amounts."
